topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany door clicked shut with a finality that scraped against my nerves. I stood in the sterile, overly quiet office, the scent of old paper and lemon polish suddenly suffocating. "Gone?" The single word felt like a physical blow, vibrating in my chest. I slammed my palm onto the desk, the sound echoing too loudly in the cavernous room. Years of shared late nights, of whispered strategies and shared victories, all reduced to this abrupt, chilling silence. I turned to the empty chair where he used to sit, my jaw tight enough to crack. How dare he just evaporate? I paced the perimeter of the room, my boots thudding a frantic rhythm on the expensive carpet. Every memory of his guidance, every correction, now curdled into a bitter acid in my throat. I needed an explanation, a justification for this sudden, brutal severance.

[story 2]

I watched the security guard lock the main entrance, the heavy metal door sealing the world out. My hands were shaking, a low, insistent tremor running up my forearms. He was gone. Just… gone. The silence in the apartment was deafening, amplifying the frantic drumming inside my own skull. I kicked at a loose floorboard, the sharp crack sounding pathetic against the backdrop of my internal turmoil. I walked over to his desk, my shadow stretching long and distorted under the harsh overhead light. I didn't shout; the anger was too sharp, too focused for noise. I picked up a stack of old blueprints, my fingers gripping the edges until my knuckles bleached white. "Where is he?" The question was barely a rasp, laced with a dangerous edge that I couldn't quite suppress, the sheer injustice of it burning behind my eyes.

[story 3]

The official notice sat on the kitchen counter, crisp and impersonal. Retirement. It was a polite, bureaucratic euphemism for erasure. I picked up the letter, the paper crinkling under my tense grip until the edges frayed. A deep, slow burn started in my gut, a heat that radiated outward, making the air around me feel thick and hard to breathe. I dropped the paper onto the granite countertop with deliberate force. I looked around the pristine, empty living space, seeing only the ghost of his presence. I didn't cry. Instead, I began to methodically dismantle the room—shoving books haphazardly, rearranging furniture with violent, jerky movements. Each action was a physical manifestation of the deep, corrosive resentment building inside me. I wanted to scream until my throat tore, to demand an accounting for this sudden abandonment.
